title: Arrays & Hashing
summary: Hash maps, frequency counts, and prefix sums are the warm-up act of **every** OA you're targeting — Microsoft Codility, Flipkart GRiD, Trilogy. You already know these patterns; the goal of this module is to make them *reflexive*, so the easy problems cost you five minutes instead of twenty and you bank that time for the graph and DP monsters later in the test.

notes.heading: Why this module buys you time
notes.body:
This is a **review** module for you — you've written hash-map code plenty of times. So the target here isn't understanding, it's *speed*: converting "I could figure this out" into "I've already coded this pattern 30 times."

Here is the recognition checklist. When a problem statement contains one of these signals, your hands should already be typing:

- **"Have I seen this value / does X exist?"** in $O(1)$ → hash set or map.
- **Counts, frequencies, duplicates, anagrams** → frequency map (or a fixed 26-slot array).
- **Sums over subarrays / "count subarrays satisfying X"** → prefix sum, usually plus a hash map of prefixes seen so far.
- **Brute force is an $O(n^2)$ double loop whose inner loop just *finds a matching partner*** → replace the inner loop with a hash lookup and drop to $O(n)$.
- **"$O(1)$ extra space" or "in place"** → index-as-hash tricks: sign-marking, swap-to-home.

> [!KEY]
> Almost everything in this module is one trade, made over and over: **spend $O(n)$ memory on a hash structure to make each lookup $O(1)$**, and an $O(n^2)$ loop collapses to $O(n)$. When the problem *forbids* the memory, the array's own indices become the hash table.

> [!EXAM] Where the minutes are
> In a 90-minute Flipkart set with 3 hards, or a Codility screen with ~2 mediums, the array/hashing problem is the one you must finish on autopilot. Every minute saved here is a minute you spend on the problem that actually decides the test.

notes.heading: Engine 1 — the "have I seen it?" map
Engine 2 — frequency counting
Engine 3 — prefix sums + the map (Two Sum, one level up)
When they forbid extra space — the array becomes the hash table
notes.body:
Some problems attach the kicker: *"$O(1)$ extra space."* That bans the map — but if the values live in a bounded range like `[1, n]`, the array's **own indices** can play the role of hash slots. Two classic moves:

**Sign-marking** (Find All Numbers Disappeared): to record "value `v` exists", flip `nums[v-1]` negative. The *sign* is your boolean, the *magnitude* still holds the original data. At the end, any index still holding a positive number corresponds to a missing value.

**Swap-to-home** (First Missing Positive): value `v` *belongs* at index `v-1`. Cyclically swap each element toward its home slot; afterwards, the first index not holding its home value exposes the answer. Each swap parks at least one element permanently, so the total work is $O(n)$ even though there's a `while` inside the `for`.

> [!WARNING] Sign tricks bite back
> Once you start flipping signs, `nums[i]` may already be negated by the time you read it. **Always read `abs(nums[i])`** when extracting the value, and remember the trick collapses entirely if the input can legitimately contain non-positive numbers — check the constraints before committing.

> [!NOTE]
> Both moves get full worked treatments in the Deep Dives tab (Disappeared Numbers' sibling **First Missing Positive** is the boss fight). Skim them now, then come back after the ladder.

> [!EXAM] Say the trade out loud
> In an interview, name the tradeoff before choosing: "hash set gives me $O(n)$ time and $O(n)$ space — do you want $O(1)$ space? Then I'll use the array itself as the hash table." Asking that question is itself worth points.

notes.heading: Pitfalls, edge cases, and how to narrate
notes.body:
The recurring bugs, collected — read this list again the night before each OA:

| Bug | Fix |
|---|---|
| Insert before query in complement problems | **Query the partner, then insert self** — reversed, elements pair with themselves |
| Missing `cnt[0] = 1` in prefix counting | Seed it — otherwise subarrays starting at index 0 vanish |
| `int` overflow on running sums/products | `long long` for prefix sums, always |
| `unordered_map` where `int cnt[26]` fits | Small bounded domain → fixed array, faster and cleaner |
| `mp[key]` **inserts** a zero entry on read | Use `.count()` / `.find()` when checking must not mutate |
| Re-reading a sign-flipped value | Read `abs(nums[i])`; verify inputs are positive first |

**Edge cases to name unprompted** (interviewers score this): empty array, single element, all-equal elements, duplicates, negatives (which kill sliding-window-on-sum), and values outside the interesting range.

> [!EXAM] The 4-step narration script
> 1. State the brute force and its cost first: "$O(n^2)$, pairwise check" — establishes a baseline.
> 2. Name the bottleneck: "the inner loop is just a lookup."
> 3. Kill it: "a hash map makes that $O(1)$, so overall $O(n)$."
> 4. Call the space/time trade explicitly and *ask* whether $O(1)$ space is required — if yes, pivot to the in-place tricks.

> [!KEY]
> Under Codility/GRiD pressure: fixed-size frequency array when the domain is small, `long long` prefix sums, and **don't over-engineer** — the plain hash-map version usually passes. Bank the saved minutes for graphs and DP.

problems.name: Two Sum
problems.note: The archetype: complement lookup in a hash map — query first, insert after.

problems.name: Contains Duplicate
problems.note: Hash set membership — the pure "have I seen it?" reflex.

problems.name: Valid Anagram
problems.note: Frequency array over 26 letters; compare the two signatures.

problems.name: Majority Element
problems.note: Frequency counting first, then learn Boyer–Moore voting for the $O(1)$-space follow-up.

problems.name: Group Anagrams
problems.note: Build a canonical key (sorted string or count signature) and let the map group for you.

problems.name: Top K Frequent Elements
problems.note: Frequency map + bucket sort by count for $O(n)$ (a heap also works).

problems.name: Intersection of Two Arrays
problems.note: Set intersection — dump one array into a set, filter the other.

problems.name: Product of Array Except Self
problems.note: Prefix × suffix products in two passes — no division allowed (see Deep Dives).

problems.name: Subarray Sum Equals K
problems.note: Prefix sum + map of prefix counts; seed `cnt[0]=1` and query before inserting.

problems.name: Longest Consecutive Sequence
problems.note: Hash set + "only walk from run starts" trick for true $O(n)$ (see Deep Dives).

problems.name: Find All Numbers Disappeared in an Array
problems.note: Index-as-hash: sign-mark `nums[v-1]` in place; read values with `abs()`.

problems.name: Set Matrix Zeroes
problems.note: Use the first row/column as your $O(1)$ marker storage — handle them last.

problems.name: Valid Sudoku
problems.note: Hash sets per row / column / 3×3 box; the skill is encoding the key cleanly.

problems.name: First Missing Positive
problems.note: Cyclic in-place placement — the array *is* the hash table (see Deep Dives).

deepdives.q: **Product of Array Except Self** — build `out` where `out[i]` is the product of every element except `nums[i]`. Division is forbidden, the array may contain zeros, and you should aim for $O(1)$ extra space beyond the output.
**Subarray Sum Equals K** — count the number of *contiguous* subarrays whose elements sum to exactly `k`. Values may be negative, so the count can't be found with a window.

**Longest Consecutive Sequence** — given an unsorted array, return the length of the longest run of consecutive integers (e.g. `[100,4,200,1,3,2]` contains `1,2,3,4` → answer 4). Required: $O(n)$, so sorting is off the table.
**First Missing Positive** — find the smallest positive integer missing from an unsorted array in $O(n)$ time and $O(1)$ extra space. Example: `[3,4,-1,1]` → `2`.

quiz.q: An OA asks: *"count the contiguous subarrays whose sum equals `k`; elements may be negative."* Which approach is correct?
options: Sliding window: grow the right end, shrink the left while the sum exceeds k
Running prefix sum + hash map counting occurrences of each prefix value
Sort the array, then use two pointers from both ends
Boyer–Moore majority voting over subarray sums
quiz.explanation: With negatives, shrinking a window doesn't monotonically decrease its sum, so the sliding-window invariant breaks and it silently returns wrong counts — it's not just slow, it's incorrect. Sorting destroys contiguity, and Boyer–Moore solves a completely different (majority) problem. The prefix-map reduction `pre[l] = pre[r+1] − k` works for any signs in $O(n)$.

quiz.q: In the one-pass Two Sum template, you move the line `seen[nums[i]] = i;` to *before* the map query. What goes wrong?
options: An element can now match itself — e.g. `nums = [3, 5]`, `target = 6` returns `{0, 0}`
Complexity degrades from O(n) to O(n²)
The function can no longer find pairs where both numbers are equal, like `[3, 3]` with target 6
Nothing — the order of the two lines is interchangeable
quiz.explanation: Insert-first means the map already contains index `i` when you query for `target − nums[i]`, so if `nums[i]` is exactly half the target it "finds" itself. Complexity is unaffected (B), and the correct query-then-insert order actually *handles* the `[3, 3]` case fine (C) — the second 3 finds the first one, which was inserted on the previous iteration.

quiz.q: In the `subarraySum` template, what does the seed `cnt[0] = 1` accomplish?
options: It prevents a crash when `cnt.find(pre - k)` misses
It makes the algorithm work when the array contains negative numbers
It counts subarrays that start at index 0 — those whose full prefix equals k
It avoids double-counting when the same prefix value occurs twice
quiz.explanation: When a prefix `pre` itself equals `k`, the matching earlier prefix is `pre − k = 0` — the *empty* prefix before index 0. Seeding `cnt[0] = 1` represents that empty prefix so those subarrays are counted. A failed `find` never crashes (A), negatives are handled by the prefix identity itself (B), and repeated prefix values *should* each count (D) — that's why the map stores counts, not booleans.

quiz.q: `longestConsecutive` has a `while (s.count(cur+1))` loop nested inside a `for` loop over the set — yet it runs in $O(n)$. Why?
options: Hash set lookups get faster as the set shrinks during iteration
The while loop can execute at most log n times per element
The set stores elements in sorted order, so runs are contiguous in memory
Walks begin only at run starts (x−1 absent), so each element is stepped onto at most once across all walks combined
quiz.explanation: The guard `if (s.count(x-1)) continue;` ensures each consecutive run is walked exactly once, from its unique smallest element — so the *total* inner-loop work over the whole execution is at most n steps. That's amortized analysis, not a per-iteration bound (B). Unordered sets aren't sorted (C) and don't speed up as you iterate (A). Remove the guard and it's O(n²).

quiz.q: Trace `subarraySum(nums = [1, 2, 3], k = 3)`. After the loop finishes, what is `ans`?
quiz.explanation: Prefixes run 0, 1, 3, 6 (with `cnt[0]=1` seeded). At `pre=1`: look up `1−3=−2`, absent. At `pre=3`: look up `0`, count 1 → ans=1 (subarray `[1,2]`). At `pre=6`: look up `3`, count 1 → ans=2 (subarray `[3]`). Two subarrays sum to 3. If you got 1, you probably forgot the `cnt[0]=1` seed — exactly the bug the template guards against.

quiz.q: You need to check whether key `k` exists in an `unordered_map<int,int> mp` **without modifying the map**. Which check is safe?
options: `if (mp[k])`
`if (mp.count(k))`
Any of these — reads never modify a map
quiz.explanation: `operator[]` default-constructs a missing key, silently inserting a `{k, 0}` entry — so options A and B both *mutate* the map on a miss (and also can't distinguish "absent" from "present with value 0"). Use `.count(k)` or `.find(k)` for pure queries. This matters in prefix-count code, where a phantom zero entry can corrupt later counts.

quiz.q: In `firstMissingPositive`, the swap loop condition includes `nums[nums[i]-1] != nums[i]`. If you drop that clause (keeping the range checks), what happens on input `[1, 1]`?
options: Infinite loop — the two equal values swap with each other forever
Wrong answer: it returns 1 instead of 2
Undefined behavior from indexing out of bounds
Nothing — the range checks alone are sufficient
quiz.explanation: At `i=1`, `nums[1]=1` is in range, so it swaps with `nums[0]` — which also holds 1. The array is unchanged, the condition is still true, and the loop spins forever. The guard says "only swap if the home slot doesn't already hold this value," which is what terminates duplicates. The range checks (B, C's concern) only keep indices legal; they can't detect futile swaps.

m01-two-sum-lookup.caption: Two Sum at `i = 1` on `[2, 7, 11, 15]`, target 9. The map holds only *earlier* elements (green). We query for `need = 2`, find it at index 0, and return — the current element is inserted only if no partner exists yet.

m01-prefix-sum.caption: The prefix-sum identity on `nums = [1,2,3,4,5]`: the shaded subarray `nums[1..3]` equals `pre[4] − pre[1] = 10 − 1 = 9`. Note `pre` has one extra slot — `pre[0] = 0` is the empty prefix.

m01-run-start.caption: Longest Consecutive on the set `{1,2,3,4,100,200}`. Only `1`, `100`, `200` are run starts (their predecessor is absent); the walk `1→2→3→4` covers each member of the run exactly once, so total work stays $O(n)$.
m01-run-start.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Walking a consecutive run only from its start"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">x is a run start iff x − 1 ∉ set</text><rect x="20" y="56" width="44" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok"/><text x="42" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-ok">1</text><line x1="68" y1="78" x2="108" y2="78" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/><rect x="112" y="56" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="134" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">2</text><line x1="160" y1="78" x2="200" y2="78" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/><rect x="204" y="56" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="226" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">3</text><line x1="252" y1="78" x2="292" y2="78" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/><rect x="296" y="56" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="318" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">4</text><text x="42" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok">start: 0 ∉ set</text><text x="226" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">walk the run once → length 4</text><rect x="460" y="56" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-muted"/><text x="482" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-muted">100</text><rect x="552" y="56" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-muted"/><text x="574" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-muted">200</text><text x="528" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">also starts — runs of length 1</text><text x="20" y="170" class="t-sm t-muted">each element is stepped onto by a walk at most once → O(n) total</text><text x="520" y="170" class="t-bold t-ok">best = 4</text></svg>

m01-home-swap.caption: First Missing Positive on `[3,4,−1,1]`: cyclic swaps send each in-range value `v` to its home index `v−1`. After placement, index 1 is the first slot not holding `i+1` — so the answer is **2**.
m01-home-swap.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 270" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Cyclic swap-to-home placement for First Missing Positive"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">send value v home to index v − 1, then scan</text><text x="20" y="90" class="t-sm t-muted">before</text><rect x="110" y="62" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="132" y="90" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">3</text><rect x="156" y="62" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="178" y="90" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">4</text><rect x="202" y="62" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="224" y="90" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">−1</text><rect x="248" y="62" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="270" y="90" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">1</text><line x1="201" y1="114" x2="201" y2="144" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/><text x="220" y="134" class="t-sm t-muted">cyclic swaps (each swap parks one value)</text><text x="20" y="178" class="t-sm t-muted">after</text><rect x="110" y="150" width="44" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok"/><text x="132" y="178" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-ok">1</text><rect x="156" y="150" width="44" height="44" class="f-bad-soft s-bad"/><text x="178" y="178" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad">−1</text><rect x="202" y="150" width="44" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok"/><text x="224" y="178" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-ok">3</text><rect x="248" y="150" width="44" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok"/><text x="270" y="178" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-ok">4</text><text x="132" y="212" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">0</text><text x="178" y="212" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">1</text><text x="224" y="212" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">2</text><text x="270" y="212" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">3</text><text x="20" y="250" class="t-sm">first index where nums[i] ≠ i + 1:</text><text x="280" y="250" class="t-bold t-bad">i = 1 → answer 2</text></svg>
